coredata.dc:description: As a new type of fluorescent agents, carbon dots (CDs) have revealed great potential in biomedical imaging applications attributed to their unique optical properties and good biocompatibility. However, engineering a versatile CDs nanoplatform integrating multifunctional imaging and therapeutic functionalities has always been an intractable challenge in targeted cancer treatment based on carbon nanomaterials. Herein, a gadolinium-doped CD conjugated with AS1411 aptamers (AS1411-Gd-CD) was prepared via facile solvothermal approach for targeted fluorescence (FL)/MR imaging guided photothermal therapy (PTT). The synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs exhibited uniform nanostructure, good biocompatibility, strong red-fluorescence emission, significant MR contrast and efficient photothermal conversion. Benefiting from these outstanding properties, the AS1411-Gd-CDs were successfully employed in tumor-specific FL/MR dual-modal imaging in vitro and in vivo. Furthermore, under the guidance of FL/MR imaging, the AS1411-Gd-CDs demonstrated significant tumor inhibition effect owing to the outstanding photothermal effect. Thus, this study provided a versatile CDs nanoplatform as a multifunctional theranostic agent for synergistic FL/MR imaging and PTT in cancer treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of Gd-CDs: (A) TEM image of Gd-CDs, inset: particle size distribution of Gd-CDs; (B) HRTEM image of Gd-CDs; (C) EDS spectrum of Gd-CDs; (D) XPS spectra of Gd-CDs, (E) C1s, (F) N1s, (G) F1s, (H) Gd4d and (I) O1s.

(A) FT-IR spectra of AS1411 aptamers, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs; (B) Zeta potential of CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs; (C) UV–vis–NIR spectra of AS1411 aptamers, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs; (D) Excitation and emission spectra of AS1411-Gd-CDs, inset: photograph of AS1411-Gd-CDs solution irradiated under UV (right) and sunlight (left); (E) Emission spectra of AS1411-Gd-CDs by changing excitation wavelength from 470 nm to 550 nm; (F) FL intensity change of AS1411-Gd-CDs for 30 days, inset: photograph of AS1411-Gd-CDs solution irradiated under UV (right) at 0 d, 15d and 30d.

Photothermal studies of AS1411-Gd-CDs: (A) Infrared thermal images of AS1411-Gd-CDs suspension of varying concentrations exposed to NIR laser for 0 ∼ 10 min (808 nm, 1 W/cm2); (B) Temperature elevation in AS1411-Gd-CDs aqueous suspensions with various concentrations over 10 min of NIR laser irradiation (808 nm, 1 W/cm2); (C) Photothermal performance of AS1411-Gd-CDs (100 μg/mL) exposed to NIR laser with different power densities; (D) Photothermal heating/cooling curves of AS1411-Gd-CDs suspensions with laser on and off state for 4 cycles (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 500 μg/mL; Heating: 300 s, Cooling: 900 s).

In vitro MR imaging properties of AS1411-Gd-CDs. (A) T 1-weighted MR images of AS1411-Gd-CDs agarose samples at different Gd concentrations; (B) Longitudinal relaxation rates (1/T1) of as a function of Gd concentrations; (C) T1-weighted MR images of 4 T1 and NIH3T3 cells treated with PBS, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, the red circles indicated the tumor cells; (D)MR signal intensity of 4 T1 and NIH3T3 cells incubated with PBS, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs.

In vitro cytotoxicity and cellular uptake behavior of AS1411-Gd-CDs: (A) In vitro cytotoxicity test of AS1411-Gd-CDs against NIH3T3 and 4 T1 cells; (B) The hemolytic percentage of AS1411-Gd-CDs to human red blood cells (PBS as a negative control and DI water as a positive control); (C) Confocal FL images of the cellular uptake of CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs by 4 T1 cells, scale bar = 20 μm; (D) Fluorescence histograms of the 4T1cells incubated with CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, 4 T1 cells treated with PBS were set as control group (red curve in the merged image).

In vitro photothermal therapy for 4 T1 tumor cells (A) Live/dead cells staining images of 4 T1 cells after various treatments (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 5 min). The cells were co-stained with calcein-AM (green) and PI (red), respectively; (B) Flow cytometric analysis of cell apoptosis after various CDs combined with NIR laser treatments determined by the Annexin V-FITC/PI assay (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 5 min); (C) Cell viabilities of 4 T1 cells after photothermal ablation with different NPs treatments (left, 0.12 mg/mL) and In vitro cytotoxicity of CDs + laser, Gd-CDs + laser, and AS1411-Gd-CDs + laser at various concentrations (right) (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 5 min), *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

In vivo fluorescence and MR imaging tests. (A) In vivo fluorescence imaging of 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice after intravenous injection of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, the red circles indicated the tumor area; (B) FL signal intensities within tumor regions at corresponding time points after intravenous injection of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs; (C) T1-weighted MR imaging of mice model pre- and post-injection of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs at various time intervals. (D) T1-weighted MRI signals of the tumors at determined time points after administration of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, the red circles indicated the tumor area.

In vivo anticancer effect in 4 T1 tumor bearing mice. (A) Schematic illustration of animal experiments design; (B) Thermal images of the tumor bearing nude mice upon different injections under the irradiation of 808 nm NIR laser for 5 min (808 nm, 1 W/cm2); (C) Temperature changes in the tumor region under 808 nm laser irradiation at various injection (808 nm, 1 W/cm2, 5 min); (D) Relative tumor volumes of the 4 T1 tumor bearing mice receiving various treatments for 14 days, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01; (E) Variation of mouse body weight over time in each group after various treatments; (F) Photographs of 4 T1 tumor bearing mice and the H&E staining of the tumor at 14 day for various groups: Saline, NIR only, Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs, Gd-CDs + NIR, AS1411-Gd-CDs + NIR, scale bar = 100 μm; (F)) Photos of tumors dissected from each group on the 14th day after photothermal treatment. 1: saline, 2: NIR only, 3: Gd-CDs, 4: AS1411-Gd-CDs, 5: Gd-CDs + NIR and 6: AS1411-Gd-CDs + NIR.
Schematic illustration of preparation of AS1411-Gd-CDs and FL/MR-guided photothermal therapy of tumor.

head.ce:title: Gadolinium doped red-emissive carbon dots as targeted theranostic agents for fluorescence and MR imaging guided cancer phototherapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a new type of fluorescent agents, carbon dots (CDs) have revealed great potential in biomedical imaging applications attributed to their unique optical properties and good biocompatibility. However, engineering a versatile CDs nanoplatform integrating multifunctional imaging and therapeutic functionalities has always been an intractable challenge in targeted cancer treatment based on carbon nanomaterials. Herein, a gadolinium-doped CD conjugated with AS1411 aptamers (AS1411-Gd-CD) was prepared via facile solvothermal approach for targeted fluorescence (FL)/MR imaging guided photothermal therapy (PTT). The synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs exhibited uniform nanostructure, good biocompatibility, strong red-fluorescence emission, significant MR contrast and efficient photothermal conversion. Benefiting from these outstanding properties, the AS1411-Gd-CDs were successfully employed in tumor-specific FL/MR dual-modal imaging in vitro and in vivo. Furthermore, under the guidance of FL/MR imaging, the AS1411-Gd-CDs demonstrated significant tumor inhibition effect owing to the outstanding photothermal effect. Thus, this study provided a versatile CDs nanoplatform as a multifunctional theranostic agent for synergistic FL/MR imaging and PTT in cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as attractive nanomaterials in biomedicine field attributed to their remarkable optical properties, good stability and high biocompatibility, etc [1]. Taking advantage of these properties, CDs could be deployed in various biomedical applications, such as bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery and nanomedicine, etc [2-4]. In early research, most of CDs are synthesized to emit blue to orange fluorescence under the excitation of UV or blue light [5-7]. However, the short excitation and emission light of these CDs may induce injury to the living system, limit the tissue-penetration depth or be interfered by the blue auto-fluorescence from the biological tissues, which greatly restricted their applicability for in vivo optical imaging [8]. Therefore, over the last decade, many efforts have been dedicated to the modulation of the fluorescence emission properties of CDs across red to near-infrared (NIR) range by doping various elements in the matrix of CDs, such as N, S, F, etc [9-13]. Till date, several groups have reported different red-fluorescence emissive CDs and some of them were successfully employed in animal imaging [14,15]. For example, Zhang’s group developed S, Se-codoped CDs with excitation wavelength-independent NIR emissions at 731 and 820 nm [16]. Yang and co-workers also reported N-doped CDs with 31% efficient red emission for in vivo imaging [17]. Although these red-emissive CDs were successfully applied in fluorescence imaging, the single modality of optical imaging still could not satisfy the urgent demand in precise cancer diagnosis.

Recently, multimodal imaging has drawn tremendous attention in diagnostic imaging to combine two or more imaging modalities into one nanoprobe, such as optical imaging, MR imaging, ultrasound imaging, PET, CT and PA imaging [18-20]. The combinations of different imaging modalities could produce comprehensive tissue information and offer complementary advantages of each imaging modality to provide more reliable and accurate detection of disease sites. As reported, most popular multimodal imaging nanoprobes are the integrations of optical and MR imaging modalities [21,22]. As one of the most powerful diagnostic techniques, MR imaging could produce high spatial resolution images for anatomical details with deep tissue penetration in a non-invasive way [23]. On the other side, the optical imaging is a non-damaging imaging technique with high sensitivity to molecular level and allows fast screening [24]. Thus, in addition to the use in optical imaging, the combination with MR imaging could enable CDs nanoplatform to acquire multiple imaging details with high sensitivity and high spatial resolution to provide more detailed anatomical or biological information about the targeted site [25].

Very recently, nanotheranostics has emerged as a promising strategy in cancer treatment by developing a smart multifunctional nanostructured material to integrate the imaging and therapeutic functionalities [18]. Normally, these multifunctional nanoplatforms could be constructed by assembling above-mentioned imaging modalities with various therapeutic agents, such as chemotherapeutic drugs, radiosensitizer, photosensitizers and immune adjuvant, etc [26-29]. This theranostic strategy enables single dose to simultaneously diagnose, kill tumors and achieve imaging guided cancer therapy [30]. As a noninvasive therapeutic approach, photothermal therapy (PTT) could powerfully and selectively ablate tumor cells that utilizes local heat generated by the NIR light-absorbing agents converting light energy to heat [1,31]. Various nanomaterials have been widely investigated as PTT agents, such as metal-based nanomaterials, carbon nanomaterials and some organic NIR chromophores [32]. Recently, carbon dots were also reported to be used as PTT agent in cancer treatment. For example, Geng et al. synthesized NIR-responsive N-O-doped CDs for efficient photothermal cancer therapy at low power densities [33].

As reported, nanoparticular systems could selectively accumulate at tumor site after long blood circulation via the mechanism named enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect [34]. However, these passive approaches still undergo several limitations ascribed to the insufficient diffusion process and the inhomogeneous permeability of vessels in the solid tumors. Therefore, active binding of nanoplatforms to specific tumor cells by targeting moiety modification proposed an effective solution to overcome the limitations of passive targeting, which highly improved the accumulation of the nanocarriers at tumor site [35]. Various targeting ligands have been applied to enhance the targeting ability of different nanoparticles, such as folic acids, RGD, aptamers and antibodies [36-40]. Among them, AS1411 aptamers display high affinity with the nucleolin expressed on breast, renal and other adenocarcinoma cells, which enables the nanovehicles to achieve high targeting specificity [40].

Herein, in this study, as shown in Scheme 1 , we synthesized Gd doped CDs (Gd-CDs) via one-step solvothermal approach for FL/MR imaging guided photothermal cancer therapy. AS1411 aptamers were utilized to conjugate on the surface of synthesized Gd-CDs to form AS1411-Gd-CDs for improved tumor targetability. At first, the AS1411-Gd-CDs were fully characterized in multiple features including morphology, physiochemical properties, optical performances, MR contrast effect and photothermal conversion, etc. Given to these properties, AS1411-Gd-CDs were successfully deployed in targeted fluorescence and MR dual-modal tumor imaging in vitro and in vivo. Furthermore, the imaging guided PTT was well investigated and exhibited significant anticancer efficacy on 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice. Finally, the excellent biocompatibility of the AS1411-Gd-CDs was also confirmed via in vitro and in vivo experiments. These combined superb properties enable AS1411-Gd-CDs to be applied as outstanding FL/MR dual-modal imaging nanoprobe and promising PTT agent in cancer theranostics.

Citric acid anhydrate (CA), urea, ammonium fluoride (NH4F), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Gadolinium chloride hexahydrate (GdCl3·6H2O), N,N’-dimethyl formamide (DMF), acetone, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and Calcein AM/PI kit were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium/Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F-12), Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), EDTA-trypsin, DPBS buffer and penicillin/streptomycin were purchased from Gibco BRL (MD, USA). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. The DNA sequence was listed as follows. NH2-AS1411: 5ʹ-NH2-GGTGGTGGTGGTTGTGGTGGT GGTGG-3ʹ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of gadolinium doped carbon dots (Gd-CDs) and AS1411 aptamer grafting

ce:section.ce:para: The Gd-CDs were prepared following the modified process reported previously [9]. Firstly, citric acid (2 g) and urea (4 g) were completely dissolved in 30 mL of DMF. Then, NH4F (0.195 g) and GdCl3·6H2O (1.5 g) were added into the above mixture following with vigorous sonication to form a homogenous solution. Thereafter, the resultant solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated to 180 ℃ and kept for 4 h and then naturally cooled down to room temperature. Afterwards, the reacted red suspension was dispersed in NaOH aqueous solution (50 mg/mL), sonicated for 5 min, and further centrifuged for 10 min at 12,000 rpm. Subsequently, the obtained black precipitate was sequentially washed by diluted HCl aqueous solution and distilled (DI) water each for three times. Finally, the Gd-CDs were obtained by centrifugation (12000 rpm, 10 min) and lyophilization to acquire purified Gd-CDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AS1411 aptamers modified Gd-CDs (AS1411-Gd-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the AS1411 aptamer grafting on the Gd-CDs was achieved by the EDC/NHS activation. Briefly, previously synthesized Gd-CDs (2 mg) was suspended in 1 mL of DI water. Then, EDC (5 mg) and NHS (3 mg) were added into the above suspension and reacted for 0.5 h at 37 °C incubation for activation. Afterwards, AS1411 aptamer solution (50 μL, 100 μM) was further added and maintained in 37 °C atmosphere for another 2 h. Subsequently, the resultant solution was centrifuged and washed thrice with DI water to remove the unreacted chemicals. Finally, the product was stored in PBS for further use.

The morphology of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs was visualized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy equipped with EDS function (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit). The particle size distribution and zeta potentials of prepared CDs were evaluated with a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments, UK). The UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra were investigated using a UH4150 HITACH spectrometer. The fluorescence properties were measured by a LS-45/55 Fluorescence/Phosphorescence Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, America). The chemical structures were analyzed by Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor27) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo escalab250Xi, USA). The amount of Gd in the Gd-CDs was investigated by an inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, Agilent ICPMS7800). The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized Gd-CDs was measured with the quinine sulfate solution in H2SO4 as standard reference.

The photothermal effect was explored on Gd-CDs aqueous suspensions under the irradiation of NIR laser by a continuous wave fiber-couple diode laser (808 nm, FC-808–10 W, Changchun New Industries Optoelectronics Technology Co. Ltd, China). The temperature changes of the suspensions of various concentrations (DI water, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25 and 0.5 mg/mL) were measured by an infrared thermal camera (FLIR E60; FLIR system, Inc.) for 10 min with 30 s intervals at different laser intensities (0.2, 0.5, 1, 1.5 and 2 W/cm2). To investigate the photothermal stability, the Gd-CDs suspension was exposed to NIR laser and naturally cooled down to room temperature for repeated 4 cycles.

NIH3T3 mouse embryonic fibroblast cells and 4 T1 mouse breast cancer cells were received from Chinese Academy of Sciences Cell Bank (Shanghai, China). The above cell lines were respectively cultured in DMEM and DMEM/F-12 medium containing 10% FBS and 100 U/mL penicillin–streptomycin. The cells were incubated in 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C.

The biocompatibility of AS1411-Gd-CDs was assessed on NIH3T3 and 4 T1 cells by standard CCK-8 assay [41-43]. Firstly, the cells were inoculated in 96-well plate and incubated with different concentrations of AS1411-Gd-CDs (0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 mg/mL) for 24 h. Then, the cells were carried out standard CCK-8 assay to evaluate the surviving cells in each cell by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm with a microplate reader. The cell viability of the cells could be calculated from the percentage of the surviving cells in each incubated well compared with the control group as the mean values of triplicate parallel tests.

The fluorescence imaging potential of prepared Gd-CDs was firstly investigated on 4 T1 cancer cells. Briefly, 4 T1 cells (3 × 105 cells/well) were cultured on the coverslips in a 6-well plate and incubated for 24 h. Then, various formulation of CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs (0.1 mg/mL) were added into each well and co-incubated with the cells for 4 h to allow cellular uptake. Thereafter, the cells were washed carefully with PBS buffer for several times and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde aqueous solution. Afterwards, the cells were further washed thrice and mounted with medium containing DAPI. Finally, the samples were observed by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, LSM880, Zeiss, Germany).

The cellular uptake of different CDs was further quantitatively explored by flow cytometry analysis. 4 T1 cells (3 × 105 cells/well) were seeded on 6-well plate overnight. Then, the cells were treated with the different formulations of CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs for 4 h for cellular labelling. After that, cells were carefully rinsed thrice with PBS buffer and detached with trypsin-EDTA solution. Finally, the cells were collected in the FACS buffer and analyzed with a flow cytometry system (AutoMACS Pro, Miltenyi Biotec).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro photothermal ablation for tumor

ce:section.ce:para: To observe the photothermal antitumor effect, live/dead cell staining was firstly conducted upon NIR irradiation. Briefly, 4 T1 cells were cultured on a 6-well plate and co-incubated with CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs for 4 h to allow uptake of cells. Afterwards, cells were washed carefully with PBS buffer and subsequently irradiated by NIR laser (808 nm, 1 W/cm2) for 5 min. Thereafter, the cells were stained with calcein AM and PI and imaged by a fluorescence microscopy.

Flow cytometry analysis was performed to investigate the cell apoptosis after photothermal treatments. 4 T1 cells were (3 × 105 cells/well) were cultured on 6-well plate for 24 h. Then, different formulations including CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs (0.2 mg/mL) were added in each well and co-incubated with the cells for 4 h, and then the cells were subjected to NIR irradiation (1 W/cm2) for 5 min. Subsequently, cells were stained with Annexin V-FITC/PI Cell Apoptosis Detection Kit and analyzed with a flow cytometry system (AutoMACS Pro, Miltenyi Biotec).

The photothermal ablation efficacy of the tumor cells was further quantitively evaluated by CCK-8 assay. 4 T1 cells were seeded in 96-well plate at 1 × 104 cells/well and incubated in 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, 4 T1 cells were incubated with PBS, CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs at different concentrations (PBS, 0.02, 0.04, 0.08, 0.12 and 0.2 mg/mL). After 24 h incubation, cells were irradiated with NIR laser at different intensities for 5 min. Upon the NIR irradiation, cells were further incubated for 24 h and then analyzed by standard CCK-8 assay to evaluate the surviving cells in each well. The cell viability could be calculated from the absorbance at 450 nm by comparing with the control group.

The MRI contrast potential of Gd-CDs was investigated on a 3.0 T GE Discovery 750 W MR system. The Gd-CDs were diluted in PBS buffer at various Gd concentrations (0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.5 mM) and placed in Eppendorf tubes of 1 cm in diameter. The MR imaging of these samples was carried out following the Spin-Echo sequence. T1 MR images of the Gd-CDs solution under various treatments and the parameters could be set as the following values: TR = 425 ms, TE = Min Full, Matrix size = 384 × 224, Field of View = 18 cm × 18 cm, Slice thickness = 3.0 mm, spacing = 1.5 mm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeted MR imaging for cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the targeted MR imaging of the prepared CDs were performed on NIH3T3 normal cells and 4 T1 tumor cells with Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs. The above cells were firstly cultured on 6-well plate and maintained overnight for cell proliferation. Then, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs of various concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5 mM) were added in each well and co-incubated for 4 h. Afterwards, the cells were washed several times with PBS buffer, trypsinized and harvested in Eppendorf tubes by centrifugation. Then, the cells were re-dispersed in 1% agarose solution for gelation. Finally, the samples were subjected to MR imaging on the previous MR system.

In this study, Balb/c mice (female, 7-week) were obtained by the Animal Center of Xuzhou Medical University. The animal study was approved by the Animal Care Committee of Xuzhou Medical University prior experiment. 4 T1 cell suspension (1 × 106 cells dispersed in 100 µL) were subcutaneously inoculated in the right flank. Animal study were performed when the tumor volume grew to about 100 mm3.

The in vivo FL imaging properties of synthesized Gd-CDs were explored by a IVIS Spectrum imaging system (IVIS Lumina III; PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA). Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs suspended in saline were intravenously injected into 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice (5 mg/kg). Then, the FL images of mice were obtained at various determined time points pre-and post-injection (1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24 h). On the other hand, for in vivo MR imaging, tumor-bearing mice upon same dose injection were subjected to T1-weighted MR imaging before and at pre-determined time points after injection according to the previous parameters.

In vivo photothermal therapy was conducted on tumor-bearing mice. The mice were randomly distributed into six groups (5 mice in each group) for the in vivo experiments. The mice were intravenously injected with different formulations: (i) Saline, (ii) NIR, (iii) Gd-CDs, (iv) AS1411-Gd-CDs, (v) Gd-CDs + NIR, (vi) AS1411-Gd-CDs + NIR (5 mg/kg). Then, 808 nm NIR laser-irradiation (1 W/cm2, 10 min) was conducted after 24 h post-injection for some groups of mice at the tumor region. The temperature of the tumor area was monitored by an IR camera. The tumor size was measured every three days and calculated as follows: V = width2 × length/2. After two weeks measurement, tumors were collected from the sacrificed mice after various treatments for further analysis.

Healthy Balb/c mice were injected intravenously with AS1411-Gd-CDs at a dose of 10 mg/kg and sacrificed at determined time points post-injection. The blood sample of each mouse was collected at 1d, 7d and 21d after injection. Blood samples from mice without any injection was harvested as control. Then, the collected blood samples were subjected to biochemistry examination and complete panel analysis with the index of WBC, RBC, PLT, HGB, MCV, MCH, MCHC, HCT, ALB, AST, ALT TP, Urea and creatinine. Furthermore, major organs were also collected from the sacrificed mice for H&E staining. The images were observed by an optical microscope.
All data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). A Student’s t-tests were used to evaluate the statistical significance of the differences (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs.
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Scheme. 1, the Gd-CDs were synthesized following a solvothermal method, and then modified with AS1411 aptamers. The morphology and nanostructure of Gd-CDs were firstly explored by a HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the synthesized Gd-CDs exhibited excellent dispersibility and uniform size distribution with an average diameter of 2.5 nm. The hydrodynamic diameter of Gd-CDs was measured by DLS measurement. As shown in Fig. S1, the DLS result for Gd-CDs exhibited a slightly larger size of 21.3 ± 3.1 nm compared to TEM result due to the existence of various functional groups on the surface of Gd-CDs. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 1B revealed clear lattice fringes in the circular structure of the Gd-CDs. The lattice spacing in the Gd-CDs was measured to be 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (001) plane of graphite carbon. It indicated that the synthesized Gd-CDs were well crystallized.

Subsequently, the chemical compositions of Gd-CDs were explored by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The EDS data in Fig. 1C displayed the presence of C, N, O, F and Gd elements in the Gd-CDs structure. The XPS spectra (Fig. 1D-I) further verified the chemical composition of Gd-CDs at typical peaks of 285, 400, 531, 686 and 145 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, F1s and Gd4d, which was consistent to the element composition determined by the EDS. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1E, the high resolution C1s XPS spectra exhibited different peaks at 290. 5, 288.9, 287.72, 286.56 and 284. 77 eV, which belonged to C–F, C=O, C–O, C–N and C–C bonds, respectively. The N1s XPS spectra displayed two peaks at 399.9 and 401.9 eV, which were ascribed to the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N, respectively (Fig. 1F). The high-resolution F1s spectra revealed typical peak at 686.3 eV attributed to the C-F bond in the structure (Fig. 1G). In addition, the high resolution Gd 4d XPS spectra revealed peaks at 142.9 and 148.1 eV (Fig. 1H). These XPS spectra results demonstrated the N, F and Gd-doped property of the Gd-CDs.

To improve the targeting ability, AS1411 aptamers were grafted on the surface of Gd-CDs. The Gd-CDs and modification of AS1411 aptamer was characterized in various aspects. As exhibited in the FT-IR spectra of Gd-CDs in Fig. 2 A, the broad peak at around 3410 cm−1 corresponded to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The peaks at 2924 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 were attributed to the –CH3 and –CH2 bonds in the structure, respectively. In addition, the peaks at 1650 cm−1, 1350 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 were assigned to the characteristic C=O, C–N and C–O stretching vibrations, respectively, which was consistent to the XPS analysis. Furthermore, after AS1411 aptamer grafting, double peak at 1650 cm−1 and 1560 cm−1 were found in the spectra of AS1411-Gd-CDs, indicating the existence of carbonyl (C=O) and amine (N–H) groups of peptide bond (–CONH) formed in the AS1411 aptamers conjugation process. Moreover, a new characteristic peak also emerged at 1040 cm−1, indicating to the O-P stretching vibration of phosphate groups in nucleic acid duplex of conjugated AS1411 aptamers. Besides, the AS1411 modification was also detected by zeta potential analysis. As shown in Fig. 2B, the zeta potentials of CDs and Gd-CDs were measured to be −38.9 and −37.6 mV, respectively, attributed to the abundant presence of carboxyl groups (–COOH) on their surface. However, after the grafting of AS1411-NH2, the zeta potential value significantly decreased to −14.3 mV due to the large consumption of carboxyl groups in the AS1411 aptamer grafting process. These results demonstrated the successful conjugation of AS1411 aptamers on Gd-CDs.

Next, the optical properties of AS1411-Gd-CDs were measured to evaluate the fluorescence performance of prepared AS1411-Gd-CDs. As presented in Fig. 2C, in the UV–vis-NIR spectrum of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, a broad absorption band could be observed from UV to NIR range with absorption peaks at 310, 510 and 980 nm. In addition, a new peak at around 254 nm indicating the AS1411 aptamer newly appeared in the curve of AS1411-Gd-CDs compared to Gd-CDs, further verified the successful conjugation of AS1411 aptamer on Gd-CDs. Besides, the fluorescence performance of AS1411-Gd-CDs was also assessed. As shown in Fig. 2D, the excitation and emission spectra exhibited the optical excitation and emission wavelengths of AS1411-Gd-CDs were 520 nm and 625 nm, respectively. Moreover, the AS1411-Gd-CDs aqueous solution displayed light pink and transparent under day light and strong red luminescence under excitation (Fig. 2D inset image). Then, we also studied the emission spectra of AS1411-Gd-CDs under various excitation wavelengths. As depicted in Fig. 2E, the emission wavelength remained stable at 625 nm but the emission intensity varied with the excitation wavelength. It demonstrated that the fluorescence emission of AS1411-Gd-CDs was independent on the excitation. As shown in Fig. 2F, the emission intensity of AS1411-Gd-CDs did not exhibit any significant change after storage for 30 days, indicating its good fluorescence stability. Furthermore, the fluorescence QY was measured and calculated to be 5.6% for the AS1411-Gd-CDs in water, which would be enough for further optical imaging application in vivo [9].

Given to the broad optical absorption of AS1411-Gd-CDs in the visible and infrared ranges, the photothermal efficacy was evaluate by irradiating NIR laser (808 nm, 1 W/cm2) to CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs suspensions at various concentrations for 600 s. As shown in Fig. S3, the temperature of DI water increased by 3.4 °C after NIR laser irradiation (1 W/cm2) for 10 min. On the other hand, the CDs suspension was heated to approximately 49.2 °C. In comparison, the temperature of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs suspensions rise to about 51.1 and 51 °C, respectively under the same NIR irradiation. The slight improved photothermal conversion of AS1411-Gd-CDs might be attributed to the metal–ligand complex structure after Gd doping in the CDs [43]. Thereafter, the photothermal properties of AS1411-Gd-CDs suspension was detailly investigated. Thermal images of various Gd-CDs concentrated suspensions were recorded to determine the actual photothermal behavior in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 3 A, color changes indicating the temperature changes in the thermal images were clearly observed in the PBS solution and AS1411-Gd-CDs aqueous suspension with increasing concentration. More specially, as exhibited in Fig. 3B, the temperature of PBS solution merely increased to 24 °C after the exposure to 808 nm laser for 10 min. Whereas, the AS1411-Gd-CDs suspensions were gradually heated by the laser irradiation with time compared with PBS buffer. The temperature elevation displayed highly concentration-dependent pattern for the AS1411-Gd-CDs suspensions, and the final temperature reached 31 °C, 48 °C, 51 °C, 63 °C and 66 °C for 10 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 250 μg/mL and 500 μg/mL, respectively after laser irradiation for 10 min (Fig. 3B). In addition, to further explore the photothermal conversion of the AS1411-Gd-CDs, a suspension of 100 μg/mL were irradiated by 808 nm laser of various power intensities. As indicated in Fig. 3C, the temperature curves revealed strong laser power-dependent effect, and the suspensions were heated to from 44 °C to 74 °C at the laser power from 0.2 W/cm2 to 2 W/cm2. These results suggested excellent photothermal effect of AS1411-Gd-CDs to efficiently convert laser energy into heat. Furthermore, the temperature curves presented no significant change after four repeated cycles of laser on-and-off, demonstrating the constant photothermal conversion behavior of the synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs (Fig. 3D). Thus, these data indicated the potential of AS1411-Gd-CDs for the application in photothermal therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro MR imaging of AS1411-Gd-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: In this work, Gd was dopped in the lattice of CDs to construct a novel MR contrast agent. Therefore, MR phantom imaging and relaxation study was performed to evaluate the contrast ability of Gd-CDs at various Gd concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the MR signals in the T1-weighted MR images became brighter and brighter along with increasing concentrations from 0.05 mM to 0.5 mM, indicating its concentration-dependent MR contrast effect. Furthermore, the transverse relaxation rate (1/T1) versus Gd concentrations was plot, which revealed a linear curve after fitting, as shown in Fig. 4B. The longitudinal MR relaxation rate (r1) was calculated from the slope of the linear curve, and was found to be 13.4 mM -1s -1. It demonstrated that the doped Gd in the CDs lattice highly shortened the longitudinal relaxation and enhanced the r1 value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro targeted MR imaging for tumor cells
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro cellular MR imaging was further carried out to investigate the specific cell-targeting ability of AS1411-Gd-CDs and their MR imaging potential for cancer cells. NIH3T3 and 4 T1 cells were co-incubated with Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs of various concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4C, no obvious difference in the low MR signal was noticed in the NIH3T3 cells treated by Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs. Moreover, the T1-weighted MR images exhibited much brighter signal at the same treatment in 4 T1 cells compared with NIH3T3 cells. Furthermore, for the 4 T1 cells, AS1411-Gd-CDs revealed significantly enhanced MR signal than the cells incubated with Gd-CDs. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4D, the MR signal intensity of AS1411-Gd-CDs groups was almost 2-fold of the signal of Gd-CDs groups in 4 T1 cells, indicating the specific targetability of the AS1411-Gd-CDs. Furthermore, the MR imaging for 4 T1 cells was also relative to the concentrations of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs (Fig. S5). Thus, these results further verified the specificity of the AS1411-Gd-CDs to tumor cells and demonstrated their applicability in MR imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro biosafety and cellular uptake study

ce:section.ce:para: Before deploying the synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs for further biological applications, the biocompatibility was firstly explored on NIH3T3 and 4 T1 cells by CCK-8 assay. As indicated in Fig. 5 A, both NIH3T3 and 4 T1 cells remained high viability above 81% after 24 h co-incubation, indicating that the AS1411-Gd-CDs induced negligible toxicity to the cells. In addition, we also investigated the hemocompatibility of the AS1411-Gd-CDs by observing the hemolysis of red blood cells with DI water, PBS and gradient AS1411-Gd-CDs solutions. As presented in Fig. 5B, no obvious hemolysis was observed for the PBS and all AS1411-Gd-CDs treated groups compared with DI water treated red blood cells. We also evaluated the hemolysis ratio of the different concentrations of AS1411-Gd-CDs, which the highest ratio of the hemolysis was found to be <5.3%. Therefore, these data confirmed the wonderful biocompatibility of the synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs for the application in biological field.

The fluorescent bioimaging potential of the various CDs was firstly investigated by the cellular uptake of 4 T1 cells. 4 T1 cells were co-incubated with CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs for 4 h, and then imaged by a confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 5C, vivid red fluorescence was observed in the cytoplasm of the cells incubated with CDs. In addition, for the Gd-CDs treated group, no notable fluorescence change was observed in the cells compared with CDs groups, indicating that Gd doping in the CDs did not induce significant influence to the fluorescence of CDs. Moreover, to improve the targetability of Gd-CDs to the tumor cells, AS1411 aptamer was grafting on the Gd-CDs. As revealed in the Fig. 5C, much stronger red fluorescence could be found in the 4 T1 cells treated with AS1411-Gd-CDs, demonstrating that the AS1411 aptamer modification could significantly improve the targeting ability and internalization of CDs into 4 T1 tumor cells. These results confirmed the capability of AS1411-Gd-CDs for the application in targeted bioimaging for tumor.

Besides, the internalization of the synthesized CDs was also quantitatively investigated by flow cytometry analysis. 4 T1 cells were treated by CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs for 4 h and then analyzed with a flow cytometry system. As presented in Fig. 5D, the CDs and Gd-CDs incubated cells exhibited similar moderate fluorescent signal compared with control group. However, the intensity was significantly strengthened after AS1411 aptamer modification on the CDs attributed to the high affinity of AS1411 aptamer to 4 T1 tumor cells. These FACS data were highly identical with fluorescent images visualized by CLSM. Thus, these results confirmed the potential of AS1411-Gd-CDs for bioimaging and targeted tumor labeling in cancer diagnosis.

The in vitro photothermal therapeutic effect was firstly explored on 4 T1 tumor cells by the live/dead cell staining assay. 4 T1 cells were incubated with CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, irradiated by the 808 nm laser and co-stained with calceinAM and PI. As depicted in Fig. 6 A, 4 T1 cells in the control (PBS) group revealed vivid green fluorescence, indicating the good living condition of the cells. Moreover, cells in the CDs, Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs without laser irradiation and PBS + laser groups also exhibited strong green fluorescence in the whole view of the images, demonstrating that CDs, Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs and laser irradiation only could not induce any actual injury to the tumor cells. However, when combined with laser irradiation, CDs and Gd-CDs efficiently brought about cell death to a certain level, which more than half of cells were stained to be red in the field. More than that, the most significant cell killing effect occurred in the cells treated with AS1411-Gd-CDs after laser irradiation, indicating that the PTT effect was markedly improved by the AS1411 modification ascribed the increased internalization of AS1411-Gd-CDs inside tumor cells.

Next, an Annexin V-FITC/PI fluorescence staining was also performed on the cells after photothermal treatments and analyzed by a flow cytometry system. As shown in Fig. 6B, more than 90% of cells were found to be distributed in the left lower quadrant for the PBS ± laser, CDs, Gd-CDs, AS1411-Gd-CDs without laser irradiation group, indicating the good living condition of the cells after the above treatments. However, after laser irradiation, the apoptosis rate achieved 70.8% and 76.9% for the CDs and Gd-CDs incubated cells, respectively, owing to their photothermal ablation to the tumor cells. The similar killing effect of CDs and Gd-CDs also confirmed that Gd doping did not greatly influence the photothermal conversion of CDs. Furthermore, the cell apoptosis rate was critically enhanced to 90.4% for the AS1411- Gd-CDs treated group owing to the higher cellular uptake. The flow cytometry data suggested that cells treated by various CDs displayed irreversible damage upon photothermal treatment and induced efficient ablation to the tumor cells.

The photothermal antitumor effect of the various CDs formulations was further quantitatively assessed by the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 6C, without laser irradiation, various CDs formulations including CDs, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs did not induced evident damage to the 4 T1 cells compared to the control group. However, in contrast, the cell viabilities decreased to about 38% and 36% for the CDs and Gd-CDs exposed to 808 nm laser, respectively, attributed to their photothermal effect. Furthermore, for the AS1411-Gd-CDs treated group, the PTT outcome was significantly strengthened, which the cell viability further declined to 20% for this group. It demonstrated that more cellular internalization of CDs would result in apparent improvement in the PTT performance. Besides, under the 808 nm laser irradiation, the PTT effect gradually increased with increase of the concentration of various CDs. In more detail, the cell viability critically fell to <9.8% for the AS1411-Gd-CDs group at a concentration of 0.2 mg/mL. Thus, these results indicated that the AS1411-Gd-CDs would be a great potential photothermal conversion agent for PTT application in cancer treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo tumor targeted FL/MR imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Given to the in vitro fluorescence and MR imaging results, we further studied the in vivo fluorescence and MR imaging feasibility of the Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs on 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice. As exhibited in Fig. 7 A, the whole body of each mouse revealed clear red fluorescence after intravenous injection of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, indicating their feasibility for in vivo FL imaging. The red fluorescence at the tumor site was stronger compared to other tissues in the body. It suggested that those Gd-CDs could preferentially accumulate at the tumor site due to the enhanced the permeability and retention (EPR) effect. Furtherly, at the tumor area, the fluorescence signals of the CDs rapidly increased after the injection of the two CDs formulations and reached maximum intensity at 4 post-injection (Fig. 7A,B). Subsequently, the FL intensity at tumor site gradually declined over time. Besides, at each time point, obviously stronger signals were observed at tumor for the AS1411-Gd-CDs injected mice compared with the Gd-CDs group. The tumoral fluorescence curves also verified that more AS1411-Gd-CDs accumulated inside the tumor after AS1411 grafting attributed AS1411 aptamer-mediated tumor targetability and permeability (Fig. 7B).

Followingly, we investigated the T1-weighted MR imaging performance at various time points after the injection of Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs. As presented in Fig. 7C, the MRI images exhibited clear and strong brightening effect at the tumor area after above administration. Consistent with the FL imaging results, the MRI signal at the tumor site also rapidly increased to reach the maximum intensity at 4 h, and then decreased with time (Fig. 7C,D). In addition, at 4 h post-injection, the tumoral MR signal intensity for the AS1411-Gd-CDs injected mice was figured to be 3.89-fold of the Gd-CDs treated group. It further confirmed the higher retention and accumulation of AS1411-Gd-CDs in the tumor sites by the active tumor targeting of AS1411 aptamer. The results demonstrated that the synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs could be an efficient dual-modal nanoprobe for in vivo FL and MR imaging.

Encouraged by the superior photothermal anticancer effect in vitro, we next moved to investigate the therapeutic performance of various synthesized CDs formulations on 4 T1 tumor-bearing mice in vivo. The animal experiments schedule was shown in Fig. 8 A. Owing to the good photothermal conversion of the various CDs formulations, thermal imaging was performed to monitor temperature elevation of tumor area of the mice models upon laser irradiation. As depieced in Fig. 8B, temperature at tumor region of all groups gradually elevated over the time of laser irradiation. In detail, as shown in Fig. 8C, tumor regional temperature of the saline group only increased by 5.2 ℃ at the end of laser irradiation. In contrast, the tumor temperature of CDs and Gd-CDs group quickly rise by 17.3 ℃ and 15 ℃, respectively, indicating their good photothermal conversion capability in vivo. However, mice injected with AS1411-Gd-CDs presented the highest temperature elevation of 27.1 ℃, which was attributed to the much more accumulation of the AS1411-Gd-CDs at the tumor region compared to the other CDs formulations.

Furtherly, to quantitatively assess the therapeutic efficacy of these CDs, we investigated the tumor growth of each group for 14 days in different aspects. As shown in Fig. 8D, for the groups treated with saline, NIR only, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs, the tumor volume rapidly grew to 9.86-fold, 9.81-fold, 9.45-fold and 9.25-fold at day 14 compared with the tumor volume at day 1. It indicated that NIR only, Gd-CDs and AS1411-Gd-CDs did not have apparent tumor anticancer effect. In contrast, when irradiated with 808 nm laser, the Gd-CDs significantly inhibited the tumor growth and the relative tumor size was reduced to 3.34-fold compared to the initial volume. More importantly, the highest inhibition of tumor was observed in the AS1411-Gd-CDs, which the tumor volume was significantly inhibited to 0.48-fold upon NIR irradiation. In addition, tumors for each group were excised at the end (Fig. 8G), which revealed similar therapeutic pattern with the outcome in Fig. 8D. Accordingly, histological analysis of H&E staining was further applied to the histopathological examination of tumor sections after various treatments. As shown in Fig. 8F, H&E images indicated that the Gd-CDs under laser irradiation induced tumor tissue injury and the tumor apoptosis was more severe in the tumor tissues with AS1411-Gd-CDs plus NIR laser irradiation. Moreover, as exhibited in Fig. 8E, during the 14-d experimental period, there were no obvious body weight loss and difference in body weight between the mice in different groups at each time point.

The in vivo long-term toxicity is of great significance for the synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs to be applied in biological field. Thus, we performed the toxicity analysis on healthy mice for 21 days by the blood panel and biochemistry analysis and the histological analysis. For the blood panel and biochemistry analysis, we chose several markers to indicate the to reveal the condition of various organs, including WBC, RBC, PLT, HCG, MCV, MCH, MCHC, HCT, ALB, AST, ALT, TP UREA and creatinine. As shown in Figs. S6 and S7, no apparent difference was observed in most of blood panel and biochemistry data during the experimental period compared to the control group at day 0 without any injection. However, the index of ALT, UREA and creatinine revealed little fluctuations in the first several days after injection in contrast with the control groups. It demonstrated that the prepared Gd-CDs may induce slight effect to the liver and kidney in a short time. However, values of all the markers remained in reference ranges during the whole test.

Furtherly, we also collected the main organs of the mice after the blood collection at 0, 1, 7 and 21 days. Then, the major organs were subjected to H&E staining for histological analysis. As presented in Fig. S8, the H&E staining of the major organ slices did not reveal apparent tissue damage and inflammation or lesion in the tissues for the experimental group with Gd-CDs injections. These results suggested that the synthesized Gd-CDs were biocompatible and safe for in vivo imaging guided therapy in cancer treatment.

In summary, a new type of AS1411 modified Gd doped CDs was successfully developed by the solvothermal method for FL/MR imaging guided photothermal therapy in vivo. The synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs exhibited high-efficient red fluorescence emission with a QY of 5.6% and broad absorbance in the range from UV to NIR wavelengths. Combining with gadolinium doped inside the nanostructure, AS1411-Gd-CDs were applied as a smart nanoprobe for tumor targeted both FL imaging and T1-weighted MR imaging in vitro and in vivo, which provided comprehensive diagnostic information with high sensitivity and superior spatial resolution. Besides, AS1411-Gd-CDs also possessed high photothermal conversion and wonderful PTT therapeutic efficacy was achieved under the guiding of FL/MR imaging. Therefore, these synthesized AS1411-Gd-CDs contributed a novel multifunctional carbon nanomaterial as a highly promising theranostic agent for tumor multimodal imaging and PTT.
